episode_title: Tropical birds’ ‘silent spring,’ and mapping people’s brains during surgery
summary: This Science Magazine podcast episode covers two main topics: a mysterious decline in tropical bird populations in Ecuador, Panama, and Brazil despite intact forests and protected status, potentially linked to climate instability and insect population collapses; and a refined method for mapping brain function during awake neurosurgery that measures response latency rather than just binary performance errors, improving the precision of surgical decision-making for tumor and lesion removal near language areas.
science_topics: Tropical bird population decline in Amazon rainforests
Insect population decline in tropical ecosystems
La Niña climate effects on avian populations
Tropical ecosystem biodiversity and niche specialization
Direct electrical stimulation (DES) of brain during awake neurosurgery
Language mapping and speech processing in the brain
Psycholinguistics and response latency in speech production
Functional localization of speech processing stages in motor cortex and temporal regions
North American bird decline linked to agricultural intensification
Dark matter galaxies detected by Hubble telescope
claims_to_note: Bird populations in Ecuador, Panama, and Brazil have declined following intense La Niña conditions in the 2010s and have not recovered despite changed climate conditions
Insect populations in tropical regions (particularly Costa Rica) are experiencing 'apocalyptic level of decline'
Tropical regions maintain stable conditions over millennia, allowing species to evolve into specialized ecological niches sensitive to climate instability
Insectivorous birds are experiencing the most severe declines in Ecuador and Brazil, while multiple dietary groups decline in Panama
Stimulation timing modulates effects on speech processing, with early stimulation affecting word-finding and late stimulation affecting motor speech planning
Current awake brain mapping protocols may miss subtle interference effects that occur without overt errors in task performance
North American bird decline hotspots correlate with high-intensity agriculture, cropland area, fertilizer use, and pesticide use
policy_relevance: Protected tropical forests in Ecuador, Panama, and Brazil under conservation management showing ecosystem stress despite protected status
NIH funding for long-term ecological monitoring and bird population studies across multiple sites
Clinical protocols for awake brain mapping during neurosurgery and patient consent procedures
Environmental health implications of agricultural intensification and pesticide use for migratory bird populations in North America
key_quotes: While perhaps not as dramatic as pesticide-induced loss, I think scientists in these areas are concerned that they are seeing sort of a replay of that in places that were supposed to be relatively safe. These are intact forests. They're in protected areas. They're not being logged.

One scientist mentioned this story of Guam and what happened when a snake got introduced there that devastated bird populations, and then that rippled through the condition of the forest because the birds were critical distributors of seeds that would help the trees grow. And so you take one significant piece out of the sort of Jenga tower that is an ecosystem, a ripples through other places.

When stimulation doesn't cause an error, does that brain region really not matter or are we just not measuring the right thing? Sometimes stimulation doesn't have an effect, but sometimes it does slow patients down, even when the responses are correct.

publications.title: Negative Emissions Technologies and Reliable Sequestration
Effects of Human-Caused Greenhouse Gas Emissions on U.S. Climate, Health, and Welfare
publications.description: The scientific community has been studying the question of how human-caused emissions of greenhouse gases are affecting the climate for well over a century. Much is known today, drawing on decades of direct observations of the Earth system and detailed research. This report summarizes the latest evidence on whether greenhouse gas emissions threaten public health and welfare in the United States. The impetus for this report was a notice of proposed rulemaking issued in August 2025 by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) indicating its intention to rescind the 2009 Finding of Endangerment and Cause or Contribute Findings for Greenhouse Gases Under Section 202(a) of the Clean Air Act. Recognizing that significantly more evidence is available today, the National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine launched this study to review newly available scientific evidence on the topics included in a Technical Support Document that EPA prepared to inform its decision-making on

publications.title: Greenhouse Gas Emissions Information for Decision Making: A Framework Going Forward
publications.description: Climate change, driven by increases in human-produced greenhouse gases and particles (collectively referred to as GHGs), is the most serious environmental issue facing society. The need to reduce GHGs has become urgent as heat waves, heavy rain events, and other impacts of climate change have become more frequent and severe. Since the Paris Agreement was adopted in 2015, more than 136 countries, accounting for about 80% of total global GHG emissions, have committed to achieving net-zero emissions by 2050. A growing number of cities, regional governments, and industries have also made pledges to reduce emissions. Providing decision makers with useful, accurate, and trusted GHG emissions information is a crucial part of this effort. This report examines existing and emerging approaches used to generate and evaluate GHG emissions information at global to local scales. The report develops a framework for evaluating GHG emissions information to support and guide policy makers about its use
